title: Verb Tenses That Matter Most
summary: Master the three tenses you will use 90% of the time in writing.

sections.heading: Simple Past — finished actions
sections.body: Use the simple past for actions that started and finished in the past. Most regular verbs add "-ed".

examples.wrong: Yesterday I go to the shop.
examples.right: Yesterday I went to the shop.

sections.heading: Present Perfect — past with a present link
sections.body: Use "have/has + past participle" for past actions that still matter now or have no specific time.

examples.wrong: I have seen that film yesterday.
examples.note: With a finished time word (yesterday) use simple past.

sections.heading: Present Continuous — happening now
sections.body: Use "am/is/are + verb-ing" for actions in progress right now or around now.

title: A, An, and The
summary: Stop guessing articles — learn the simple rules.

sections.heading: a vs. an
sections.body: Use "a" before a consonant SOUND and "an" before a vowel SOUND — it is about sound, not spelling.

sections.heading: the — something specific
sections.body: Use "the" when both you and the reader know exactly which thing you mean, or when it is unique.

examples.wrong: I saw a sun this morning.
examples.right: I saw the sun this morning.
examples.note: There is only one sun — it is unique.

title: Subject–Verb Agreement
summary: The verb must match the subject in number.

sections.heading: Singular vs plural subjects
sections.body: A singular subject takes a singular verb (often ending in -s); a plural subject takes the base verb.

examples.note: Plural subject, no -s on the verb.

sections.heading: Watch out for words between subject and verb
sections.body: Ignore phrases between the subject and verb — agree with the real subject.

examples.wrong: The box of apples are heavy.
examples.right: The box of apples is heavy.

title: Making Plurals Correctly
summary: Most plurals add -s, but several common words don't.

sections.heading: Regular and -es plurals
sections.body: Add -s to most nouns; add -es after s, x, z, ch, sh.

sections.heading: Irregular plurals
sections.body: Some nouns change completely and must be memorised.

examples.note: "people" is the usual plural of person.

title: Their, There, They're (and friends)
summary: Master the homophones that trip up almost everyone.

sections.heading: their / there / they're
sections.body: their = belonging to them; there = a place; they're = they are.

examples.wrong: Their going to they're house over there.
examples.right: They're going to their house over there.

sections.heading: your / you're
sections.body: your = belonging to you; you're = you are.

sections.heading: to / too / two
sections.body: to = direction/infinitive; too = also/excessively; two = the number 2.

title: In, On, At — Time and Place
summary: Pick the right little word for time and location.

sections.heading: Time
sections.body: at + clock time; on + days/dates; in + months, years, longer periods.

sections.heading: Place
sections.body: at a point; on a surface; in an enclosed space.

examples.wrong: The book is on the drawer.
examples.right: The book is in the drawer.

title: If-Clauses (Conditionals)
summary: Talk about possibilities, hypotheticals and regrets.

sections.heading: First conditional — real future
sections.body: If + present, will + base verb. Used for likely situations.

examples.wrong: If it will rain, I will stay home.
examples.right: If it rains, I will stay home.

sections.heading: Second conditional — unreal present
sections.body: If + past, would + base verb. Used for imaginary situations.

examples.wrong: If I was rich, I will travel.
examples.right: If I were rich, I would travel.
examples.note: Use "were" for all subjects in this pattern.

title: Apostrophes: Its vs It's
summary: Use apostrophes for contractions and possession — but not plurals.

sections.heading: its vs it's
sections.body: it's = it is / it has. its = belonging to it (no apostrophe).

sections.heading: Don't use apostrophes for plurals
sections.body: To make a normal plural, just add -s. No apostrophe.

title: Capital Letters
summary: Capitalise the right words — no more, no less.

sections.heading: What to capitalise
sections.body: Capitalise the first word of a sentence, the pronoun "I", names, places, days, months and languages.

examples.wrong: on monday i will visit london.
examples.right: On Monday I will visit London.

title: Commas, Periods, and the Comma Splice
summary: Punctuation controls the rhythm and clarity of your sentences.

sections.heading: The comma splice
sections.body: Two complete sentences cannot be joined by only a comma. Fix it with a period, a semicolon, or a comma + conjunction.

examples.wrong: It was late, I went home.
examples.right: It was late, so I went home.
examples.note: Add a conjunction (so, and, but) after the comma.

examples.wrong: She loves to read, she reads every night.
examples.right: She loves to read; she reads every night.
examples.note: A semicolon links two related complete sentences.

sections.heading: Commas in a list (the Oxford comma)
sections.body: A comma before "and" in a list can prevent confusion. Be consistent.

examples.wrong: I invited my parents, a clown and a magician.
examples.right: I invited my parents, a clown, and a magician.
examples.note: Without the last comma, your parents become the clown and magician!

title: Run-ons and Sentence Fragments
summary: Every sentence needs a subject, a verb, and a complete thought.

sections.heading: Fragments — incomplete sentences
sections.body: A fragment is missing a subject, a verb, or a complete idea. Join it to a full sentence.

examples.right: I went to bed early because I was tired.
examples.note: A reason on its own is not a sentence.

sections.heading: Run-ons — too much in one
sections.body: Two complete sentences crammed together need a period, semicolon, or conjunction.

examples.wrong: I love writing it makes me happy.
examples.right: I love writing. It makes me happy.

title: Building a Strong Paragraph
summary: One idea, clearly developed — the PEEL method.

sections.heading: P-E-E-L
sections.body: Point: state your main idea. Evidence: give an example or fact. Explain: say why it matters. Link: connect to the next idea or your overall argument.

sections.heading: One idea per paragraph
sections.body: If your paragraph drifts to a second topic, start a new paragraph. Readers use paragraph breaks to follow your thinking.

sections.heading: Topic sentences
sections.body: Begin most paragraphs with a sentence that signals what it is about. A reader skimming your topic sentences should grasp your whole argument.

title: Structuring an Essay
summary: Introduction, body, conclusion — give the reader a map.

sections.heading: The introduction
sections.body: Hook the reader, give brief context, and end with a clear thesis stating your main argument.

sections.heading: Body paragraphs
sections.body: Each body paragraph develops one supporting point with evidence and explanation, then links back to the thesis.

sections.heading: The conclusion
sections.body: Restate the thesis in fresh words, summarise your key points, and end with a final thought — never introduce new evidence.

title: Writing a Clear Email
summary: Professional emails are short, polite and easy to act on.

sections.heading: Subject and greeting
sections.body: Write a specific subject line and open with a greeting such as "Dear Ms Lee," or "Hi Sam,".

examples.right: Subject: Request to reschedule Friday's meeting
examples.note: A specific subject gets a faster reply.

sections.heading: Body and close
sections.body: State your purpose in the first line, keep paragraphs short, and close politely: "Best regards, …".

title: Show, Don't Tell
summary: Let details reveal emotion instead of naming it.

sections.heading: Replace labels with evidence
sections.body: Instead of stating a feeling, describe what the reader would see or hear.

examples.right: Her hands trembled as she reached for the door.
examples.note: The detail makes the feeling real.

examples.right: Clothes spilled off the chair and mugs crowded every shelf.

title: Precise Word Choice
summary: Specific words paint a sharper picture than vague ones.

sections.heading: Swap vague words for precise ones
sections.body: Words like "good", "nice" and "thing" carry little meaning. Choose a word that says exactly what you mean.

examples.right: The soup was rich and smoky.

title: Write With Power: Cut the Clutter
summary: Strong writing is lean. Remove what does not earn its place.

sections.heading: Prefer strong verbs
sections.body: Replace weak verb + noun combinations with a single strong verb.

examples.wrong: She made a decision to leave.
We had a discussion about it.

sections.heading: Cut filler words
sections.body: Words like "really, very, just, actually, basically" rarely add meaning. Delete them or use a stronger word.

sections.heading: Prefer the active voice
sections.body: Active voice is usually clearer and more direct than passive.

examples.wrong: The ball was thrown by John.
examples.note: Passive is fine when the doer is unknown or unimportant.

title: Linking Words That Make You Sound Fluent
summary: Connectors guide the reader through your logic.

sections.heading: Adding & sequencing
sections.body: furthermore, moreover, in addition, first / second / finally.

sections.heading: Contrast
sections.body: however, nevertheless, on the other hand, whereas, although.

examples.wrong: I was tired. But, I finished.
examples.right: I was tired; nevertheless, I finished.

sections.heading: Cause & result
sections.body: therefore, consequently, as a result, thus, hence.
